channel.title: NYT > Most Recent Headlines
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Para ejercitarte, solo necesitas un parque y una banca
item.description: Todo lo que necesitas para esta rutina es una banca y un poco de pasto.
item.dc:creator: Alyssa Ages and Theodore Tae

item.title: Todavía admiro a Michael Jackson. Esta es la razón
item.description: He sido seguidora del cantante toda la vida, algo que a veces no puedo defender ni siquiera ante mí misma.
item.dc:creator: Nekesa Mumbi Moody

item.title: How Germany Misjudged Trump’s Anger on Iran
item.description: After Chancellor Friedrich Merz upset President Trump with criticism of the war, he offered no public sign he believed Mr. Trump’s threats to pull troops were serious.
item.dc:creator: Jim Tankersley
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

media:content.media:description: President Trump speaking with Chancellor Friedrich Merz of Germany in the Oval Office in March.

item.title: How Do You Name the Greatest Songwriters? Much Debate.
item.description: In its latest issue, The New York Times Magazine sings the praises of 30 artists who were chosen by music experts and Times critics.
item.dc:creator: Dori Gray

media:content.media:description: The director and videographer Joshua Charow and the writer Danyel Smith sat with Mariah Carey for an interview last August.

item.title: Today’s Spelling Bee Hints for May 2, 2026
item.description: Feeling stuck on today’s puzzle? We can help.
item.dc:creator: New York Times Games

item.title: Spirit Airlines, a Pioneer of Low-Fare Flights, Shuts Down
item.description: Spirit once upended the industry by offering very low fares but was in its second bankruptcy in two years after years of struggle.
item.dc:creator: Niraj Chokshi

media:content.media:description: Spirit Airlines was such a disruptive force in its industry that other airlines felt compelled to lower their fares when it announced that it would start flying to one of their airports.

item.title: La realidad y la ficción en ‘Michael’
item.description: Verificamos los datos en varias escenas de la nueva película biográfica del Rey del Pop, Michael Jackson.
item.dc:creator: Steve Knopper

item.title: Mexican Governor Accused by U.S. of Aiding Drug Cartel Steps Down
item.description: U.S. prosecutors accused Rubén Rocha Moya of protecting the powerful Sinaloa Cartel in exchange for bribes and political support, setting off a national scandal.
item.dc:creator: Jack Nicas

media:content.media:description: Rubén Rocha Moya, the governor of the Mexican state of Sinaloa, said he had decided to take “a temporary leave of absence.”

item.title: Carlos III lanza críticas sutiles a Trump en visita a EE. UU.
item.description: Analistas en Reino Unido interpretan la visita como un ejercicio de crítica indirecta, cargada de ironía y cortesía real.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear
item.category: Charles III, King of the United Kingdom
Camilla, Queen Consort of the United Kingdom

media:content.media:description: El presidente Trump con el rey Carlos III en la Casa Blanca el martes.

item.title: La IA ahora ayuda a los médicos a tomar notas de sus pacientes
item.description: Las aplicaciones que registran las visitas ganan popularidad, pero conllevan problemas de privacidad y precisión.
item.dc:creator: Simar Bajaj

item.title: South Korea’s Cherry Blossoms Attract a Connoisseur: The Japanese
item.description: Cherry blossom viewing, or hanami, is a cherished rite in Japan. Some Japanese are also doing it in South Korea.
item.dc:creator: Jin Yu Young and Hisako Ueno

media:content.media:credit: Woohae Cho for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: The Bulguksa Temple in Gyeongju, South Korea, in April.

item.title: Quote of the Day: One Thing Uniting Bannon and Bernie: Worries About A.I.
item.description: Quotation of the Day for Saturday, May 2, 2026.

item.title: Learning Muay Thai Can Allow Visitors to Stay in Thailand Longer
item.description: Visitors can apply for a five-year visa if they take lessons in “soft power” activities like the Thai martial art. Training does not have to be grueling, and sparring is optional.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Yong and Lauren DeCicca

media:content.media:description: Instructors leading a Muay Thai training session at a gym in Bangkok.

item.title: A 5-Month Wait and a 50% Fail Rate: It’s Hard to Get a British Driving License
item.description: The average wait for a test in England and Scotland has hit 22 weeks, up from five weeks before the pandemic, leading to frustration and attempts to jump the line.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Castle

media:content.media:credit: Andrew Testa for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Grace Reynolds, right, taking a driving lesson with Dean Batchelor in March in London. “It feels like I’ve got to pass — otherwise Christmas will be probably the next time,” Ms. Reynolds said.

item.title: Not Even Japanese Bathhouses Are Immune From Shocks of Iran War
item.description: High fuel costs are imperiling the Japanese sento, a long-declining industry that remains a vital social lifeline for the country’s isolated elderly.
item.dc:creator: River Akira Davis, Kiuko Notoya and Kentaro Takahashi

item.title: Winning X or O
item.description: Hannah Slovut-Einertson’s latest themeless puzzle takes us on a solving safari.
item.dc:creator: Caitlin Lovinger

media:content.media:description: Mwah! Those are some awesomely cute little ossicones, baby.

item.title: Trump Endorses Andy Barr for Senate as Musk-Backed Candidate Exits Race
item.description: The candidate in Kentucky, Nate Morris, said he would be joining the Trump administration. He met with the president a day before his announcement.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk and Theodore Schleifer

media:content.media:credit: Jon Cherry for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Representative Andy Barr, a Kentucky Republican, was endorsed by President Trump on Friday in the Senate race to succeed Senator Mitch McConnell.

item.title: Corrections: May 2, 2026
item.description: The following editors’ note and correction appeared in print on Saturday, May 2, 2026.

item.title: Narges Mohammadi, Iranian Nobel Laureate, Is Hospitalized
item.description: Narges Mohammadi, a prominent human rights activist, was taken to a hospital after collapsing following a heart problem in prison.
item.dc:creator: Farnaz Fassihi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

media:content.media:description: Narges Mohammadi poses in an undated photo provided by her family. The Nobel laureate has suffered from heart ailments for years, according to her family.

item.title: Cuba desafía a EE. UU. en las celebraciones del Primero de Mayo
item.description: El gobierno de la isla conmemoró el Día Internacional de los Trabajadores frente a la embajada de EE. UU. en La Habana. El acto fue una oportunidad de mantener las apariencias durante la peor crisis en casi 70 años de historia del régimen.
item.dc:creator: Frances Robles

media:content.media:credit: Lisette Poole para The New York Times
media:content.media:description: El viernes por la mañana comenzaron las celebraciones del Primero de Mayo en La Habana.

item.title: Federal Judge Blocks Plan to End Deportation Protections for Yemenis
item.description: A Supreme Court court decision involving similar deportation protections for Haitians and Syrians could have implications for Yemeni migrants.
item.dc:creator: Ana Ley and Ashley Ahn
item.category: Asian American Legal Defense and Education Fund

media:content.media:description: Judge Dale E. Ho of Federal District Court in Manhattan said that the government had ignored the law when it tried to deport people vetted to receive Temporary Protected Status.

item.title: Georgia Governor Sets Date for Special Election to Fill David Scott’s House Seat
item.description: The winner will serve out the rest of Representative David Scott’s seat in Congress after the longtime lawmaker’s death last month.
item.dc:creator: Rick Rojas

media:content.media:description: Georgia Governor Brian Kemp speaks during a news conference at the State Capitol last month.

item.title: Kennedy Consulted With Scandal P.R. Firm While Seeking Cabinet Nomination
item.description: An associate of the firm, which has been accused of running a smear campaign against the actress Blake Lively, promised to suppress negative stories about Robert F. Kennedy Jr., records show.
item.dc:creator: Debra Kamin and Santul Nerkar

media:content.media:credit: Demetrius Freeman for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Robert F. Kennedy Jr. suspended his presidential campaign in August 2024, and President Trump later tapped him to be health secretary.

item.title: N.I.H. Reinstates Employee Put on Leave After Criticizing Trump Research Cuts
item.description: Jenna Norton had filed a whistle-blower complaint claiming that the agency leadership had retaliated against her.
item.dc:creator: Sheryl Gay Stolberg

media:content.media:description: Jenna Norton in Bethesda, Md., last year.

item.title: Cornell President’s Car Bumps Into Students After Confrontation Over Gaza
item.description: After a debate over the war, students say the university president hit them with his vehicle. He says he was the victim in the incident.
item.dc:creator: Santul Nerkar and Cole Louison

media:content.media:description: Cornell University students videotaped the school’s president as he backed up his vehicle.

item.title: Why U.S. Officials Are in Venezuela Right Now
item.description: U.S. officials visited Venezuela after the first direct flight between the two countries since 2019. Our White House correspondent Tyler Pager describes how the main message of the trip was, in the words of a White House adviser “drill, baby, drill.”
item.dc:creator: Tyler Pager, Melanie Bencosme and Todd Heisler

item.title: Eric Swalwell Was Proficient in Social Media. So Were His Accusers.
item.description: Eric Swalwell used social media to boost his image and meet women, accusers said. Some of those same women used apps and influencers to join forces years later.
item.dc:creator: Madison Malone Kircher

media:content.media:description: Eric Swalwell in 2019, during a brief run for president. Last month, he left office after sexual misconduct allegations.

item.title: U.S. to Withdraw 5,000 Troops From Germany, Pentagon Says
item.description: Officials announced the decision after President Trump expressed annoyance with the German chancellor’s remarks about the Iran war.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes and Helene Cooper
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces

media:content.media:description: Members of the Army participated in a military exercise in Hohenfels, Germany, on Thursday.

item.title: Federal Appeals Court Temporarily Halts Abortion Pill Access by Mail
item.description: The court order, in a lawsuit by the state of Louisiana, pauses a Food and Drug Administration regulation that greatly expanded access to the abortion pill mifepristone.
item.dc:creator: Pam Belluck

item.title: Southern States Move to Redistrict Under Weakened Voting Rights Act
item.description: Republican-led legislatures in Tennessee and Alabama will reconvene in the coming days. Unlike in Tennessee, however, a new map in Alabama will require Supreme Court action.
item.dc:creator: Emily Cochrane and Abbie VanSickle

media:content.media:description: Gov. Kay Ivey of Alabama said on Friday that she would summon lawmakers for a special session to adopt a new congressional map under the newly weakened Voting Rights Act.

item.title: Trump Tells Congress Why He Doesn’t Need Its Authorization for the Iran War
item.description: In letters to the House and Senate, the president asserted that the hostilities had “terminated,” in an apparent attempt to avoid having to seek congressional approval.
item.dc:creator: Erica L. Green and Megan Mineiro
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces

media:content.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: President Trump spoke to reporters before boarding Marine One at the White House on Friday.

item.title: David Rivera, excongresista de Florida, es condenado de hacer lobby por Venezuela
item.description: La petrolera estatal venezolana contrató a la consultora de David Rivera por 50 millones de dólares para influir en miembros del Congreso y de la Casa Blanca.
item.dc:creator: David C. Adams and Patricia Mazzei

media:content.media:description: El exrepresentante estadounidense David Rivera en el Edificio de Justicia Federal James Lawrence King de Miami, en marzo.

item.title: Trump Says He’s ‘Not Satisfied’ With Iran’s Latest Proposal
item.description: Also, Spirit Airlines prepares to shut down. Here’s the latest at the end of Friday.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Cullen

media:content.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: A government-organized march in Azadi Square in Tehran on Wednesday.

item.title: Netflix Plans First Wide Theatrical Release With ‘Narnia’
item.description: The company is breaking from its longstanding policy of releasing movies exclusively on its streaming service.
item.dc:creator: Nicole Sperling

media:content.media:credit: Nina Westervelt for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Greta Gerwig, the director of “Narnia: The Magician’s Nephew,” at the Academy Awards in 2024. Netflix said it would give “Narnia” an exclusive theatrical release starting Feb. 2.

item.title: What the Maine Senate Race Feels Like on the Ground
item.description: The anti-establishment mood is palpable in the state, home to one of the country’s top Senate contests. But Senator Susan Collins has some strengths.
item.dc:creator: Katie Glueck

media:content.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times, Sophie Park for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: From left, Senator Susan Collins, Gov. Janet Mills and Graham Platner

item.title: Gov. Jeff Landry Signs Law Blocking Ex-Prisoner Calvin Duncan From Taking Office
item.description: Calvin Duncan had been exonerated and elected as criminal court clerk in New Orleans. Gov. Jeff Landry eliminated the role as part of an effort to reshape the city’s judicial system.
item.dc:creator: Rick Rojas

media:content.media:description: Gov. Jeff Landry of Louisiana outside the White House last year.

item.title: On May Day Cuba’s Communist Leaders Deliver a Defiant Tone to the U.S.
item.description: In front of the American Embassy in Havana, the Cuban government held its annual International Worker’s Day celebration as the United States sought to end the communist ruling system.
item.dc:creator: Frances Robles

media:content.media:credit: Lisette Poole for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: May Day celebrations took place in Havana early Friday morning.

item.title: Bard College’s President, Leon Botstein, Will Retire After Epstein Revelations
item.description: The president, Leon Botstein, who had run Bard for 50 years, faced scrutiny over his connections to Jeffrey Epstein.
item.dc:creator: Vimal Patel

media:content.media:credit: Arden Wray for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Leon Botstein on the Bard College campus in Annandale-On-Hudson, N.Y.

item.title: Student Driver Plunges Bus Into River Seine
item.description: Over 100 rescue workers fished out a bus from the River Seine in Juvisy-sur-Orge, a commuter town near Paris, on Thursday after a bus driver in training veered it into the river.
item.dc:creator: Jamie Leventhal

media:content.media:description: Divers working on a submerged bus in Juvisy-sur-Orge, near Paris, on Thursday.

item.title: Seoul-Born Artist Shakes Up Jazz With Traditional Korean Music
item.description: DoYeon Kim, a Seoul-born musician, has become an in-demand collaborator for some of the biggest innovators in jazz and improvised music. Her work is steeped in traditional Korean and Western classical music.
item.dc:creator: Jorge Mitssunaga and Vincent Alban

item.title: Human Remains Found in Tampa Bay Are Identified as Missing Grad Student
item.description: A former University of South Florida student has been charged in the murders of Nahida Bristy and Zamil Limon, doctoral students who were reported missing last month.
item.dc:creator: Remy Tumin

media:content.media:credit: Douglas R. Clifford/Tampa Bay Times, via Associated Press
media:content.media:description: Investigators in Tampa, Fla., on April 24, after a suspect was arrested in the killings of two University of South Florida doctoral students who had been reported missing.

item.title: Thermos Recalls 8.2 Million Jars and Bottles After Customers Are Injured
item.description: The company received reports that the stoppers on some of its products had “forcefully ejected,” striking customers and causing permanent vision loss in a few cases, safety regulators said.
item.dc:creator: John S.W. MacDonald

item.title: Alabama Governor Calls Special Session to Adopt New House Maps
item.description: Gov. Kay Ivey said a map that would give Republicans an additional House seat cannot be enacted without Supreme Court action, but she wants to be ready if that happens.
item.dc:creator: Emily Cochrane and Abbie VanSickle

media:content.media:description: Gov. Kay Ivey of Alabama said on Friday that she would summon lawmakers for a special session to adopt a new congressional map under the newly weakened Voting Rights Act.

item.title: Fact-Checking Claims on Political Violence After Latest Trump Assassination Attempt
item.description: Republican commentators have argued, incorrectly, that political violence is largely a left-wing problem. Liberals have countered by citing the threats made against former President Obama.
item.dc:creator: Linda Qiu
item.category: Center for Strategic and International Studies

media:content.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: President Trump spoke after a shooting at the White House correspondents’ dinner on Saturday. Prosecutors charged the gunman with attempting to assassinate Mr. Trump.

item.title: Secret Service Avoids Hearings for Now on Correspondents’ Dinner Shooting
item.description: Security questions linger nearly a week after a gunman stormed a security checkpoint at the annual dinner.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Nehamas and Eileen Sullivan

media:content.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: President Trump and the first lady, Melania Trump, at the White House Correspondents’ Association dinner in Washington in April.

item.title: Takeaways From Week 2 of Testimony in the Harvey Weinstein Trial
item.description: Jessica Mann, who accused Mr. Weinstein of raping her at a hotel in 2013, is testifying against him for the third time. It seems to be taking a toll on her.
item.dc:creator: Hurubie Meko and Kate Christobek

media:content.media:description: Harvey Weinstein’s third trial for an accusation that he raped an actress more than a decade ago hasn’t had as much media attention as the previous trials.

item.title: Voters Sue Over Louisiana Governor’s Move to Delay Primary
item.description: Legal challenges are mounting over the decision to suspend the state’s May House primary after a Supreme Court ruling found that the congressional map was unconstitutional.
item.dc:creator: Nick Corasaniti and Emily Cochrane

media:content.media:credit: Hilary Scheinuk/The Advocate, via Associated Press
media:content.media:description: Gov. Jeff Landry, a Republican from Louisiana, suspended the state’s House primary, which was scheduled for May 16, to draw new congressional districts. Voting rights groups have sued.

item.title: Flashback: Your Weekly History Quiz, May 2, 2026
item.description: Can you sort 8 historical events?

item.title: Charlie Kirk’s Former Security Chief Sues Candace Owens for Defamation
item.description: The lawsuit focuses on remarks made by Ms. Owens, a right-wing podcaster, about Mr. Kirk’s killing, highlighting a growing tension among factions of the American right.
item.dc:creator: Ken Bensinger

media:content.media:credit: Rebecca Noble for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Candace Owens at AmericaFest, an event hosted by Turning Point USA, in Phoenix in 2022.
